speaker: interviewer
text: We-- OK, we know about personal possessions. You didn't have any personal--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. That's nothing because you never had--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a spoon and a tin can.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We didn't ask about-- all right. I'm just going to-- just get to a few questions, you know, that may spark some more--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're at tape 3. And we're interviewing Mr. George Scott. I just want to go back to your experiences in the concentration camps and to find out-- were there people that influenced you, and also, what the conditions were like inside the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, my tendency is to pick up clues that you say-- and take me other places than what your question implies. But I will try and stick to the question. People that influenced me inside the camp-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the interpersonal relationships between the inmates, or the other Jews, or the other people there, were increasingly more and more isolated. There were practically no interpersonal relationships that I am aware of. People were drawn into their own world. And whatever energy or life or spark of life they had, they spent it on themselves. And it's like a, a 'going inside oneself' experience. People heard what we had to do-- lining up, going to get food. And then you went into your own world. There was hardly any conversation that I recall. It was just waiting, waiting, waiting, and, and observing one's own breath. And, and in my own case, I thought of God a lot, but not in a religious sense, but in a-- in a-- in a-- as a power source-- as a-- as a-- as a-- as some-- as some-- something, someone, some entity, of which I felt a part of. And, and that-- I am sure that has played a role in the fact that I did survive. In terms of how we lived, we slept on concrete floors. There were wooden posts inside the barracks. There was a chimney in the middle of the barrack. It was a long building, maybe 100 feet long and maybe 50 feet wide. These are-- I never thought about this-- just as I recall them and try and picture them. And there was a '3 feet by 3 feet' chimney running along horizontally on the floor that separated the barracks into two halves. There was a small room as you entered, which I suppose belonged to the-- to the Blockalteste, which was the leader of the block. And he was responsible for the numbers, and who-- we were piled in there. There were wooden-- square, wooden beams supporting the roof. The roof was maybe 7 feet high-- a V-shaped roof. And as we got in there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somebody said, sleep, you know. We had to go to sleep. And then we just all scoot down. And we fitted in like sardines. And so my knees were in somebody else's knees. And, and we laid on the stone. And, and, and, and the day was finished. The interesting thing was that when someone turned, everybody [LAUGHS] had to turn, because there was no way. And, and, and we did. And it got to be a reflex action. But in terms of [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] mental activity or spiritual or physical activity-- beyond the fact that one tends to salivate and thank God the stomach contracts when it doesn't get food-- there was just this notion that one was breathing. And there was another day. And we had to line up for Appell again. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Role models in the camp-- I was amazed about my mother's brother, Uncle Sandor-- my great-uncle. But he never-- I never-- he never brought me any food or anything like that. He and his brother were very close. But beyond any question, had he not forced another person into this place-- and thereby saving my life-- today, I would not have a family. And I, I can-- on one hand, I feel dreadful to, to live, in a manner of speaking, two lives-- the person who died, and my own. And it's, it's, it's a notion that a normal person can't understand. But it did happen. It's, it's the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by the guards in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had very little contact with the guards. At every Appell time, which was every day, we were standing there for hours. A, a guard walked and marched by and counted us. And then we were dismissed. In Auschwitz, we-- in Birkenau, nobody worked. It was a transit camp. And now, in, in, in Kaufering, in Lager 1, before I-- after I got thrown out of the kitchen, but before I went to Landsberg, to Lager I-- I worked with a Kommando on the railroad. We were picking up stones from the railings. I don't know what we did. There were lighthouses, switching stations-- I don't know what. We were just carrying-- picking up stones. That's what we did. That was the job. And some of them were friendly. And sometimes, they allowed us to build a little fire so we could warm our hands. It was-- it was one day after another. And they read their newspapers. We had no idea of what was going on in the world. They had a paper called I guess it means 'the paper that watches after the people,' if I try to translate it. My German wasn't ever that great. But I did learn a bit of German. And I studied German in school prior to the camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One incident stands out in mind. It was in Kaufering, in Lager 3, where a person was stealing potatoes. And there were big signs that anybody gets caught will be killed. Anyhow, he was captured. And he was put between the electrified wire and the little single wire that ran on the inside. And he had to stand there, with a potato in his mouth, for like two, three, days. We could all see him. He wasn't killed. And as I told you before, this commandant put me in the kitchen. So he saved my life. And he was still the commandant. And after the war, I found out that this commandant was hung by the inmates in the camp. So it's, it's, it's, it's really a, a, a very difficult thing for me to, to stand in judgment. Apparently, this person with the potato in his mouth survived, then hung him after the war for having been punished. It's, it's-- you know, there are a lot of gray areas that are-- I think, for heroism and greatness, a human must have an opportunity. And then he must feel equal to it. And some take that opportunity. And many don't. Many don't even know that there is an opportunity. I was still not old enough to really, really know what I know today. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it's an incident that stands out in my mind. The other thing is singing and bringing back the dead. It seemed bizarre to me-- going to work singing. And, and generally, the younger SS guards were much more brutal than the older people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea, while you were in the camps, what happened to your grandmother, grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: By then I did, but not on the train, not, not, not even while they were telling us which way to go. I had no idea. And nobody did. We thought we were going to take-- be taken to a place where there'd be work for everyone, and we'll be better treated. And that's that. And that was the pleasant thing that we all wanted to believe, in spite of the horrible stories that people whispered about-- of people being shot, people being asked to dig their graves, and then they were shut into it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was not unknown. But I certainly never believed it, not until when I saw that amazing movie. And, and, and, and like, like a cold wave coming over me, it was precisely like that, when you see those people coming in with their suitcases and giving their names proudly-- Rosenberg, Wasserman-- and walking away with their paper stamped. And when you see the-- two months later-- the suitcases being empty, thrown out of the window, and these people are like nothing-- like flotsam and jetsam, like nothings.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and, and, and the world goes on. And the little voice inside every one of us-- certainly inside me, and all of us-- keeps asking questions, keeps wondering why, keeps wondering how could we make it better? How could something like this gone on and how-- excuse me-- how a thing like this must never, ever go on. Going back again, you said that you were quite sick. What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as we got to-- I didn't know it was Dachau. We got in this place at night. And we heard some-- by, by then, we all knew-- like, through seven layers of fog-- we knew that the war was coming to an end. And there was some commotion in the morning. And I ran to the-- to the-- to the fence. And by then, the fence was not electrified and put the-- I-- you know, and I grabbed the fence. And I saw soldiers coming in. There were about three or four corpses on the-- on the right side of us as we were standing in a group. They were German soldiers. And I saw a man with something on his back coming closer and closer to me. And I was there, waving. I didn't know what the thing on his back was.

speaker: interviewer
text: But about five years ago, there was an article in the 'Isn't it time to bring Nazi war criminals to justice?' My wife says, weren't you in Dachau? I never looked at stuff like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, I never talked to my children about this, not until recently, because I didn't want to weaken them. And you know, it couldn't possibly contribute to their-- I was going to say 'predicament,' but I'll change that to 'opportunities.' And, and, and when I looked at the picture, [LAUGHS] I couldn't believe my eyes. There I am, waving! So I wrote to the writers. And they sent me a big glossy, which you'll be able to see later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I get sick? Yeah. I always used to-- somehow, weird, bizarre way-- be afraid of dying. And that same day, as I was standing there, we went into a place. There were three layers of bed. There was also a huge, a huge stack of corpses lying behind the group that I was waving to the-- I think maybe 8 feet high, just every which way, just as you see them on pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, I went into my bunk. And as I am-- I remember approaching the bunk. And then it wasn't sore. It wasn't painful. Everything just melted away. And the next thing I knew, I was in a hospital, in an SS hospital, where all the doctors and the nurses were the same as-- looked after the SS. The patients were moved to another hospital. And here, these people were doing their best to keep us alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And about five weeks later, I remember having to learn to walk again. I had typhoid, or typhus, fever-- I don't know. But there I was, alive and, and wondering. And the beat goes on. Which way is up? Were you sick actually before they came, just struggling to keep on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- well, apart from what I told before-- the acid coming out of me for hours, standing in-- and I had this little-- excuse me-- this little tin cup that I spat into because it was-- it would just keep coming, the acid. I remember some people, grownups, saying that hey, if you had some milk, that wouldn't be a problem. Yes, we-- you know, this is not serious. But it was pretty uncomfortable. Excuse me. And to this day, I have acid stomach if I don't watch what I eat. But apparently, it hasn't killed me. And after, after I learned to walk, I remember there was a Dutch nurse. And I really got very fond of her. And then I found out that she had an American boyfriend. And I remember being jealous. It was a weird feeling. And she just kind of humored me, and-- there, there-- there, there. But I, I didn't-- I took that very seriously. One of the first wounds of romance-- there, there were many afterwards. [LAUGHS] And, and we were-- excuse me-- we were taken to a place called Feldafing. And it was a, a Displaced Person camp. And there were a lot of people there from different camps and perhaps even from Poland and Russia, who had-- who, after the war, came there with the idea of emigrating. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: About September, the first part of September, we just got on the train with a bunch of other Hungarians. And I went back. And I wondered-- would anybody come back? And nobody came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the doctors of my-- one of my aunts-- [? Vera, ?] Margo's daughter-- survived. And she still lives in Kosice. She's 10 years older than I. And my aunt was in Budapest, Aunt Bertha, who, by now, started working for the orphanage. So all the orphans now know her and love her-- or knew her, because she died about four years ago-- as Bertha [? Nanny. ?] But by the time this happened, I left the orphanage. Anyhow, I went back to the orphanage as if nothing has happened. Nobody asked me questions. I now was a big boy. The little guys looked up to me. I had privileges. I used to sneak out at night to go dancing. And the teachers were in collusion with me. It made me feel really important, like I'm part of the establishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did you change as far as-- you have just finished going through your experiences, coming back to find that very few people have survived. Had your attitude changed? Had your personality changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I don't think so. I-- you know, when you're in a camp for 11 months, the way I have been, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one learns to call shit, 'shit.' And, and, and, and, and it didn't come too easily to me. But that was life. As, as Forrest Gump says, 'Shit happens.' But I always try to, to learn-- and it has helped me a great deal-- that many things in life is nothing personal. It could be this way. It could be that way. It could be a lot worse. And yes, it could be a lot better, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what astounds me always is the possibility that every individual has to make their lives closer to what they would like it to be. And, and I think everyone-- I'm sure everyone has an inborn sense of fairness. What is fair? And what is unfair?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a lot of things in this world are unfair. But the kind of conditions you and I are talking about were grossly bizarre. They weren't unfair. They were-- they were criminal. They were-- they were unconscionable from any standpoint.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point in time did you find out how many Jewish people had died during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, strangely enough, the Hungarians said, [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN], which means, 'More came back than left.' So you can see how popular we are. Again, I don't know why. Approximately 650,000 Hungarian Jews were, were killed. And with the exception of Budapest, where Wallenberg was so active-- and I was not there. But many people from the orphanage-- a boy called [? John ?] Balko, B-A-L-K-O. He played the piano beautifully. I admired this boy. Everybody called him, [HUNGARIAN], 'the crazy one.' But, but he played beautifully. And I wanted so much to make music. And I, I still am trying because music, to me, is, is, is a wonderful way of speaking a language that everyone understands.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got back to the orphanage. I-- in 1947, someone came back from Germany-- who knew one of my teachers-- and said, this teacher says, I'll go back with you. And I said, I'll-- can I tag along? They didn't say yes. They didn't say no. I tagged along.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came back to Germany-- and with different papers, in 1948. The Jewish Congress paid for me. And I came to Canada. I wasn't 18 yet. Under 18, really-- even with my own papers-- you didn't need a visa. And they were very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sticklers about, about what nationality and, and, you know, Hungarians, no. Poles, no. Russians, no. Anyhow, I managed with somebody else's papers. I came as Moses [NON-ENGLISH]. But eventually when I became a citizen, I started working for Ontario Hydro down the line. First, I worked in the bush for three years in Mattawa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyhow, I don't know in what detail do you want me to talk about that, or whether this is the proper time to talk about it. I arrived in Halifax. And I guess two or three boys from the ship-- we came on the ship called Two or three boys were taken to families in Glace Bay, Nova Scotia. And I cannot tell you the feeling of seeing those white churches, those houses on the road as we were driving. It was such a feeling of surreality, of peace, as, as if-- as if Europe had been, you know, the underside of a camel's behind. I hope-- I don't wish to offend my, my people who depend on camels a great deal-- or something really bad. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I stayed in Glace Bay with a family called [? Begin. ?] They had a very sick boy. And they thought it would help if a boy came who was not sick. It wasn't very pleasant. Thank God this boy, incidentally, is alive and has a store in, in Ottawa today. I never met him. And from the [? Begins, ?] another family took me by the name of Shore. And I was with them some months. And after that, I came back to Montreal. I went to Sir George Williams High School to get my junior matriculation finished. And there-- there was a boy there who got the richest person's daughter into trouble in Mattawa. And she was going to have a baby. She did have her baby. And they were going to go back to Mattawa. And I was with another fellow, also from Glace Bay, also from the same ship, by the name of George [? Davy ?] [NON-ENGLISH]. And he-- this fellow-- his name was [? Vic ?] [? McCormand-- ?] wanted to borrow some money to get back to Mattawa. So we lent him $60. And we had an offer. Fellas, come on down, we got a job for you, $30 a week, which was, like, $12 a week more than what we were earning to mind a powerhouse. So we lived in the bush with a French-Canadian family. Of course, we didn't say we were Jewish. And we were there three years. We saved our money. And one day George came in with a Cadillac car. And, and, and I said, I didn't want the car. So we separated. I had $1,800 when I left Mattawa and came to Toronto. I got a job with Ontario Hydro. And in 1954-- I came to Toronto in '52 from Mattawa. In '54, on the street, I had a very offensive friend who was bragging-- oh, I shouldn't say this-- about this girl. And I felt sorry about this girl ever-- even before I met her. Anyhow, we got-- we met. And she loved poetry. And her name was Ruth Levstein. And after that, this fellow had nothing to brag about because we got married in five weeks. And she gave me three beautiful children. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never looked back. The rest is history. And unfortunately, my wife died in 1978. She was 43. And then, of course, I remarried. And I have an enchanted life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. OK. We'll stop here. We have time for pictures. You, you haven't given me five minutes, right? Oh. Time? Yeah. Can we go back to when you were in Canada-- if anybody in Canada talked about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] What a wonderful question. You know, the first things in Montreal when I came-- when we left-- George and I left Glace Bay and came to Montreal. And both of us started working in the clothing trade. And there were-- there were a lot of French Canadians in that trade, working alongside us. And one of the marvelous, uplifting, astounding discoveries I made-- that the French Roman Catholics were also circumcised. Because that-- you know, as a Jewish boy in Europe, you, you, you, you didn't have a chance if you were circumcised. And the very first thing I, I felt-- I'm safe. There's other kids who are circumcised, people like myself. And they're not Jewish. It's just an aside. Now, I don't know to what extent I have answered your question in terms of relevance to what people thought about the war. We tried to forget about the war. There is something in, in human nature-- there is certainly something in my own nature-- and I would say it's a good aspect-- that it tries to look for the lark aspect, the, the fun, the bizarre, the, the joyous. The macabre goes nowhere. And, and having survived what I had survived personally-- and I can really only speak of myself-- I knew that that kind of thinking is a dead end. And I often speculated and wondered about my own mother. Because I, I, I am 64 years old. I still feel ripped off. I didn't have someone who, as a child, who had me in her arms. And it's-- it-- it's, it's kind of lurid for me to speak of this right now. But I thought that if my mom hadn't been so terrified-- if, if she-- a sense of humor is so wonderful. I mean terror, I am sure-- Hitler did not invent terror. Human existence had always entailed an undesirable or, or worse aspect. And people have always had to make the best of where they were, who they were, and where they hoped to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, so I, I-- I looked for the-- if not for the positive, then, then for the humorous. And, and even though I had told you a dreadful tale which happened to me, there were heartening aspects of it. Like the guard giving me-- I am me. I only know me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I may be looking at you and looking even at my kids. But really, I have a glancing acquaintance with myself and less with others, even though observation does help for us to kind of position ourselves in terms of the rest of humankind. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I find that, that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the war, in many ways, apart from being a personal pain, is a giant embarrassment to me. I feel really embarrassed for the Germans-- for people, for people. I always looked at the guards. I even looked at Mengele as an extension of myself. And, and there are a couple of things. It's really a fantasy. But I will tell you, for whatever it's worth. My, my grandfather's mother was the daughter of the very famous Rabbi Jungreis. It's a well-known, very religious, even today, Orthodox Jewish family. And there are some rabbis today by the name of Jungreis. And I just wondered if somehow, maybe his godliness and his goodness, through his genes, I had inherited something that will survive. And what-- how that connects to the concept of God, I don't know. And I wouldn't spend too much time trying to identify it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What-- the other thing is that, that taking that as, as part of me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I look out through my eyes, I see people. And I assume that they're very much like myself. Like-- and perhaps because of my unusual deprivation at birth of a father, I, I, I, I had a-- an, an inordinate tendency to, to look for affection, to look for love from others. And when you take that beyond, to give what I didn't have. And in that sense, I have a chance to understand myself. In terms of thinking about the war and what people thought of the war then, I have always been profoundly grateful for the Jews who put in their $5 and $10 and $20 to the Congress, who made it possible that hundreds of us kids could come and start a new life and let new roots. And, and-- so that there is a tomorrow for us and an opportunity to continue to be, to continue to give-- which, per force of our natures, we must give. Anything that one doesn't give, one doesn't have. I think there is a natural tendency in us to use what we've got in, in any area, in any part of our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if I've answered your question, really. I tried. And, and I tried to go around it.
